verdict.safeSubRising: The water is below {limit} cm, but has started rising. You can still cross safely — leave before the last departure time.
verdict.safeSubFalling: The water is below {limit} cm and falling. There is plenty of time to cross the causeway.
verdict.cautionSub: The road is still passable, but the water reaches {limit} cm again at {time} — there is no longer enough time ({duration}) for a safe crossing. If you are already on the road, continue calmly across.
verdict.cautionLine1NoNext: Wait for the next low tide
verdict.unsafeSub: The causeway is flooded or about to flood — the road goes under water at about {flood} cm. Wait for the next low tide.
verdict.unknownSub: We cannot reach the measuring station right now. Do not cross based on this page — check locally.
verdict.levelNow: The water is {cm} cm right now
verdict.floodsAt: The water reaches the road at {time}
verdict.stormBanner: Weather & wind: {cm} cm above the astronomical tide, factored into the forecast.
verdict.staleBanner: The latest measurement is {duration} old — showing the DMI forecast instead. Be extra careful.

retur.bodyOk: Leave Mandø by {time} at the latest — the same deadline applies in both directions.

road.title: The road right now
road.caption: Cross-section of the road. The roadbed floods at about {road} cm. Falling water is safe to cross below {falling} cm; while it's rising, you must finish crossing while the water is still {margin} cm below flooding.

windows.title: When can I cross?
windows.intro: These are safe departure times — leave within the green period.
windows.none: No safe crossing windows in the forecast period. This usually means a storm is pushing the water up — check again later.
windows.amberNote: Amber: the road is not flooded yet, but there is no longer enough time for a safe crossing — do not set out.
windows.tooShort: Only {duration} — too short for a safe crossing
windows.showMore: See further ahead · up to 7 days
windows.extendedNote: Showing up to 7 days ahead. Beyond that the water level cannot be predicted reliably enough — weather and wind shift the windows. Check again closer to your travel day.

chart.title: Water level — past 6 h and next 30 h

dive.title: Dive deeper
dive.calcTitle: How the last departure is calculated · {day}
dive.basisBody: Right now's assessment is based on the measured water level from the Mandø station. Future windows are based on DMI's forecast, including weather and wind — which is why confidence drops the further ahead you look.
dive.fact1: Mandø can only be reached via the Låningsvej causeway at low tide — typically 2 safe windows a day.
dive.fact2: The crossing takes 20–{max} minutes by car.
dive.fact3: Never set out when the water is rising close to the limit.

explainer.title: Never seen a tide before?
explainer.whatTitle: The sea moves twice a day
explainer.whatBody: Twice every day the sea rises (high tide) and falls (low tide), pulled by the moon. At Mandø the difference is about 2 metres — enough to completely cover the road at high tide.
explainer.roadTitle: The road is sometimes under water
explainer.roadBody: Mandø is reached by the Låningsvej, a gravel causeway across the seabed. At low tide it is a normal road; at high tide it disappears under the sea. This is normal and happens twice a day.
explainer.windBody: Strong westerly wind can push the water much higher than the tide table says, and keep the road flooded all day. That is why this page uses live measurements, not just the tide table.
explainer.ruleBody: Never start crossing when the water is rising. If you are unsure, wait for the next window or join a guided tractor bus tour from the Vadehavscentret.

footer.disclaimer: This service is a guide for visitors. Weather and tide can change conditions — you are responsible for judging safety before crossing. Call 112 in an emergency.
footer.source: Live data: Danish Meteorological Institute (DMI) & Danish Coastal Authority, measuring station at Mandø.

demo.banner: Demo mode — synthetic tide data, not real measurements.

login.title: Admin sign in
login.error: Sign in failed. Check your email and password.
login.notAdmin: This account has no admin access.

rules.title: Thresholds
rules.intro: These knobs decide when the site shows green, amber and red. Levels are in cm relative to DVR90 (the Danish height reference), as measured at the Mandø station.
rules.safeMaxFallingHelp: While the tide is falling, the road is safe as soon as the level is at or below this — it only gets lower from here, so there's no time pressure.
rules.floodMarginHelp: While the tide is rising, aim to finish the crossing while the water is still this many cm below the flood point. The last safe departure is timed so the whole crossing stays below (flood point − this margin). The crossing time is the other safety knob.
rules.cautionMaxHelp: The road is fully under water at this level.
rules.crossingHelp: How long it takes to cross in the worst conditions. Used to work out the last safe departure.
rules.bufferHelp: Added on top of the crossing time for a margin of error.
rules.minWindowHelp: Windows shorter than this are hidden — too short to safely cross and return.
rules.windAdjustHelp: When on, the forecast comes from DMI's DKSS storm-surge model, which already includes wind and air pressure — so upcoming windows reflect the actual weather forecast, out to about 5 days ahead. When off, the forecast uses DMI's plain astronomical tide table only (no weather). Either way, the current water level always comes from the live measurement. Keep this on.
rules.invalid: Flood point must be at or above the falling passable limit, and the flood margin must be between 0 and the flood point.
rules.previewState: With these values the site would now show: {state}

health.title: Data health
health.surgeHelp: Difference between the measured level and the astronomical tide table — wind pushing water in (+) or out (−).
health.noData: No data — has the fetch job run?
health.timePrecisionNote: Forecast times (e.g. when the water reaches the road) are computed at 5-minute resolution, so they can read up to ~5 min late versus the exact crossing — well within DMI's forecast uncertainty.
